narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection from law school stings, especially when you've set your sights on becoming a lawyer and tackling that bar exam head-on as a full-time student. The prediction model we use to assess applications looked at your profile and estimated a 44% chance of not passing the bar, which tipped the scales against admission—it's a tough call, but one rooted in patterns from thousands of similar journeys. What stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 1, placing you at the very bottom of your class that year; it was the strongest signal pulling toward this outcome, suggesting challenges in sustaining strong performance as law school ramps up. Right behind that was your LSAT score of 21, which the model saw as a key hurdle, as lower scores like yours have historically correlated with tougher bar prep ahead. Your Year 1 decile of 2 added to that picture, showing early struggles that didn't fully turn around. Coming from a middle-income family influenced it too, as the model notes that without extra resources, the grind can feel steeper for students in your bracket. Your background as a Black female applicant played a role as well, reflecting broader trends where systemic barriers have made the path rockier despite your determination. Even your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.0, while respectable, couldn't fully offset these, pulling gently toward the riskier side in the model's view. Together, these pieces painted a story of someone with heart and full-time commitment, but facing an uphill battle based on where you've ranked academically so far. It's not the end of your story—this is just one snapshot, and with targeted steps like LSAT prep or grade-boosting strategies, you could rewrite it. You've got the drive; now it's about building the profile that matches. Hang in there—we're rooting for you.
